short.headline: India's electricity still mostly coal, but clean power rises
short.dek: Coal provided 71% of electricity in 2025, yet clean sources surpassed a quarter for the first time.
short.body: In 2025, India generated 2,083 TWh of electricity. Coal did most of the heavy lifting at 1,474 TWh (70.8%). Clean sources, hydro, nuclear, solar, wind, and bioenergy, supplied 555 TWh (26.7%), a record share. Gas contributed just 2.3%. While coal remains the bedrock, the mix is shifting. Installed solar capacity has skyrocketed to 97 GW, and renewable share hit a modern high. However, surging demand means absolute coal generation still grows. The grid is getting marginally cleaner per unit, but total emissions rise.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: For the average Indian, it means the fan, the mobile charger, the fridge, most of that still runs on coal. But slowly, the power behind your plug is getting a little greener. Rooftop solar and giant wind farms are going up really fast. The challenge is, we're using more and more electricity every year. So even though the share of clean power is growing, we're still burning more coal than ever. The good news? The big push in solar and wind tells us the next decade could look quite different. For now, if you turn on a light, it's likely coal-powered, but the green shift is real and picking up speed.
macha.soWhat: Coal still does most of the work, but the green shift is real and accelerating.

article.title: How does India generate electricity?
article.standfirst: A data-led look at India's electricity mix in 2025: from coal's dominance to the rise of renewables.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## Where did India's electricity come from last year?

In 2025, coal generators produced 1,474 terawatt-hours of electricity, or 70.8% of the total. That is nearly three-quarters of our power. Fossil fuels as a whole (coal plus a small amount of gas) accounted for 73.4% of generation. Clean sources, hydro, nuclear, solar, wind, and bioenergy, supplied 554.8 TWh, or 26.7% of the mix. Total electricity generation met demand of 2,082.82 TWh.

A terawatt-hour is a billion kilowatt-hours, enough to power a medium-sized city for a year. This stacked bar shows the single year snapshot: coal dominates, but clean sources are no longer marginal. Hydro alone contributed 178 TWh, the largest clean component. Gas remained tiny at 49 TWh (2.3%). The chart answers the page question directly: India's electricity is still mostly coal. But the 2025 mix is more diverse than it used to be, even compared to a few years ago. Seasonal shifts, however, are not visible here; the monsoon bumps up hydro temporarily, but the annual picture hides that variability.

## How have coal, clean power and gas changed over time?

While the snapshot tells you what happened last year, the trendline reveals the story. Over the past two decades, India's total electricity demand has ballooned, from 573 TWh in 2000 to over 2,082 TWh in 2025. Coal generation has grown to meet most of that demand, but clean power has also grown absolutely, even if its share remained modest. In 2025, clean sources generated 555 TWh, up from a much smaller base in 2000. Gas generation has stayed relatively small, hovering around 49 TWh.

The chart groups many clean sources together: hydro, nuclear, solar, and wind. Separately, solar has been the fastest riser, but aggregated here, its acceleration is hidden. The key insight: coal has not been displaced; instead, we have built onto it. The grid has expanded to serve more homes and industries, and both coal and renewables have added to the mix. The challenge is that demand growth is so fast that even rapid renewable additions haven't yet reduced coal's absolute generation. In fact, the gap between coal and clean generation in TWh is wider now than ever before.

## What has India built? Installed capacity by source

This chart shows the engines India has installed, not the actual electricity they produce. Installed capacity is the maximum potential output, measured in million kilowatts. By the end of 2024, total installed capacity had reached 531.46 million kilowatts, over 15 times the 34.38 million kW of 1980.

Look at the lines: fossil capacity (mostly coal plants) stands at 315 million kW. But renewable capacity has surged, particularly solar. Solar capacity exploded from essentially zero in 2000 to 97.38 million kW in 2024. Wind capacity climbed to 48.16 million kW. Together, renewables reached 204.11 million kW. This is the hardware side: the power plants ready to generate.

However, a coal plant can run around 70-80% of the time, while solar farms only produce when the sun shines, giving them a much lower capacity factor. So a solar capacity addition of 97 GW doesn't mean it will generate nearly as much as 97 GW of coal. That is why the generation mix still heavily favours coal. But the rapid solar build signals a future pivot, and the rising renewable capacity line is the most dynamic element on this chart.

## What share of electricity actually comes from renewables?

Renewables, including large hydro, supplied 24.1% of India's electricity in 2025. That is up from 19.8% in 2024, a sharp jump driven partly by strong hydro generation (178 TWh). The longer view shows a U-shaped history: back in 1985, renewables contributed 27.8% of electricity, driven entirely by large hydro. As thermal power expanded in the following decades, the share fell to about 14% in the mid-2000s. Only recently has it climbed back, thanks to solar and wind additions.

This chart tracks that share from 1985 to 2025. The line dipped then rose. The definition of renewables here includes large hydro, which some frameworks exclude. If large hydro were removed, the share would be lower, perhaps in the teens. Nevertheless, the direction is now upward, and the 2025 figure is a new high for the modern era. It shows that the grid is becoming greener, even if coal still rules. The jump from 2024 to 2025 is the single largest annual increase in the series, a sign of accelerating change.

## Is the grid getting cleaner per unit?

Carbon intensity measures how many grams of CO2 are emitted for each kilowatt-hour of electricity produced. In 2000, India's average intensity was 740 gCO2/kWh. By 2025, it had fallen to 670 gCO2/kWh, a drop of about 9%. This is progress: the grid is becoming less polluting per unit of electricity.

The line chart shows a gentle downward trend over 25 years, with occasional bumps. The reduction comes from greater efficiency at coal plants, a larger share of renewables, and more hydro. However, a falling intensity does not mean total emissions are falling. Because total electricity generation more than tripled from 573 TWh to 2,083 TWh, absolute CO2 emissions from power generation rose enormously. The lower per-unit number is like a car getting better mileage, but if you drive many more kilometres, total fuel use still goes up. India is driving much more, and that's the tension captured by this metric: we are cleaning up the grid per kilowatt-hour, but we are producing so many more kilowatt-hours that the total climate impact continues to grow.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: This page answers how India generates electricity, examining the mix from coal to solar and measuring how clean the grid has become.

selectedDataPoints.reason: Reveals the overwhelming dominance of coal in absolute terms.

selectedDataPoints.label: Solar electricity installed capacity, latest
selectedDataPoints.reason: Highlights the breakneck speed of solar build-out.

pullQuotes.quote: Coal generated 70.8% of India's electricity in 2025.
Clean sources crossed the 25% mark for the first time in the modern era, hitting 26.7%.
Solar installed capacity reached 97.4 million kW in 2024, up from nearly zero in 2000.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Imagine a 100-watt light bulb running for 10 billion hours. That's a terawatt-hour. It's a massive energy unit used to measure national electricity production.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Every generation figure on this page is in TWh because India's annual output is in the thousands.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of a car's engine rated at 100 horsepower. It can produce that power at full throttle, but you rarely drive at full speed. Installed capacity is the maximum power a plant can deliver, measured in megawatts. It doesn't tell you how much electricity it actually makes.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The capacity charts show the rapid addition of renewable hardware, but generation numbers reveal the real output.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: If you made a fruit salad, the mix tells you that 70% is apples, 25% is bananas, etc. Here, it's the proportion of electricity from coal, gas, hydro, nuclear, solar, and other sources.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The page question directly asks for this breakdown.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Suppose every time you take a taxi, you pay a CO2 'tax' in grams. Carbon intensity is the grams of CO2 emitted to get one kilowatt-hour of electricity. A lower number is cleaner.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It measures whether our grid is getting less carbon-heavy even as consumption grows.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A solar panel only works when the sun shines, maybe 20% of the day on average. Its actual output over a year is a fraction of its theoretical maximum. That fraction is the capacity factor.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: It explains why solar's huge capacity doesn't yet translate into the same share of generation.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A single stacked bar for the latest year is most readable on mobile.
Lines show the trend clearly; using 3 lines avoids clutter on a phone screen.
Multiple lines let readers compare build-outs directly; sparse ticks keep it mobile-friendly.
A single line trend is the simplest way to show a share over time.
A falling line is intuitive for improvement; works well on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: Where India's electricity came from last year
chartExplainers.takeaway: Coal generated 70.8% of India's electricity in 2025, while clean sources accounted for 26.7%.
chartExplainers.detail: The chart stacks each source's terawatt-hours into a single bar, revealing the generation mix at a glance. Coal's segment towers at 1,474 TWh. Clean power (hydro, nuclear, solar, wind, bioenergy) reaches 555 TWh. Gas adds 49 TWh. Together, fossil fuels made up 73.4% of the electricity we used. This is the most direct answer to the question: coal is the heavy lifter, but clean sources are now more than a quarter of the story. Hydro alone contributed 178 TWh, the biggest clean source. The total bar represents 2,083 TWh of demand, a number that has grown enormously over the years.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It directly answers the page question with the latest annual breakdown.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each segment's share of the bar height shows its percentage of total generation; the bar equals 100% or 2,083 TWh.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse share percentages with absolute generation; a small share like gas (2.3%) still represents 49 TWh.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A stacked bar works on mobile if the legend is clear and segments are labeled.

chartExplainers.title: Coal, clean power and gas over time
chartExplainers.takeaway: Coal generation has climbed relentlessly to meet soaring demand, while clean power has also grown steadily.
chartExplainers.detail: The three lines show annual generation in TWh. The coal line starts dominant and expands rapidly; in 2025 it delivered 1,474 TWh. The clean line has risen too, reaching 555 TWh, but its growth rate has accelerated only recently. Gas remains a flat line near 50 TWh. Total electricity demand nearly quadrupled from 2000 to 2025, pulling all sources upward. The important pattern: renewable and nuclear additions are not replacing coal; they are complementing it to serve a larger grid. The gap between coal and clean generation has widened in absolute terms, even as the clean share edges up.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It reveals the evolution of the fuel mix and the persistent dominance of coal even as clean sources rise.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Follow the top line (coal) to see its expansion; compare it with the middle line (clean) to gauge relative growth.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume the clean line is closing in on the coal line; in absolute terms, the gap is growing.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Three lines with distinct colors and thick strokes remain legible on a small screen.

chartExplainers.title: What India has built: installed capacity by source
chartExplainers.takeaway: Solar capacity has shot up from zero to 97.4 million kW in two decades, but fossil capacity still leads at 315 million kW.
chartExplainers.detail: Installed capacity represents the maximum potential output. Total capacity has grown from 34 million kW in 1980 to 531 million kW in 2024. Fossil fuels (mostly coal) account for 315 million kW. Renewable capacity, driven by solar, has surged to 204 million kW. Solar alone went from almost nothing in 2000 to 97.4 million kW. Wind reached 48.2 million kW. These build-out lines are steep, especially after 2010, reflecting aggressive renewable targets. But a megawatt of solar capacity does not equal a megawatt of coal in actual generation because of lower capacity factors. This explains why the generation mix still tilts heavily coal, even though renewable capacity is closing in on fossil capacity.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows the faster-moving capacity build-out, which feeds into future generation.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The lines show installed capacity over time; the solar line rises sharply from 2010 onward.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't equate capacity with generation; solar panels produce less electricity per megawatt of capacity than a coal plant.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a multi-line chart with sparse tick marks to avoid clutter.

chartExplainers.title: Renewables' share of generation
chartExplainers.takeaway: Renewables supplied 24.1% of India's electricity in 2025, a notable jump from 19.8% the year before.
chartExplainers.detail: The single line tracks the percentage of generation from all renewables, including large hydro. It started high at 27.8% in 1985, driven entirely by hydro, then fell as thermal capacity expanded, bottoming around 14% in the mid-2000s. Since then, it has climbed back, boosted by solar and wind additions. The 2025 figure of 24.1% represents a new post-1980s high, with hydro alone contributing 8.6 percentage points (178 TWh). The definition of renewables here includes large hydro, which some international tracking excludes. If we removed large hydro, the share would be lower, but the upward trend is real and accelerating.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It isolates the headline green yardstick: the percentage of electricity that is renewable.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line moves from left to right; higher means a greater share of renewables.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't interpret the 1980s peak as a 'good old days', it was almost all hydro, and the grid has since diversified.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A single line is easy to render on a phone; label the start and end points clearly.

chartExplainers.title: The grid is getting cleaner per unit
chartExplainers.takeaway: Carbon intensity dropped from 740 gCO2/kWh in 2000 to 670 in 2025, a sign of cleaner electricity.
chartExplainers.detail: This metric counts grams of CO2 emitted per kilowatt-hour generated. A lower number means less pollution for the same amount of electricity. India's intensity has declined gradually, with a noticeable dip in 2025 thanks to higher hydro and renewable generation. From 740 in 2000 to 670 in 2025, the improvement is about 9%. However, because total generation more than tripled over the same period, total CO2 emissions from power have risen drastically. Think of it as a car that now emits slightly less per kilometre, but is driven many times more kilometres. The falling line is progress, but the absolute scale of emissions is still increasing.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It measures progress in lowering emissions per kilowatt-hour, the key climate metric.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line trends downward; a steeper drop would indicate quicker decarbonization.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't mistake a lower per-unit intensity for falling total emissions; they have gone in opposite directions.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with a clear downward slope is intuitive on a small screen.

sourceNotes: Ember: Yearly electricity data (generation, demand, carbon intensity).
OWID: Renewable share of electricity production.
caveats: The 2025 generation snapshot doesn't show seasonal variability; hydro output fluctuates with monsoons.
Installed capacity is not generation; solar and wind have lower capacity factors than coal, so capacity numbers overstate their actual energy contribution.
The renewable share includes large hydro, which some definitions exclude; removing large hydro would lower the share.
A falling carbon intensity does not mean total CO2 emissions are falling; because demand grew more than threefold since 2000, absolute emissions from electricity have risen substantially.
lockedNumbersUsed: Fossil fuels electricity installed capacity, latest

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India generates electricity primarily from coal, which supplied about 71% of generation in 2025, though clean sources are growing and now provide over a quarter of the total.
requiredConcepts: Define TWh as the total energy unit
Explain the difference between installed capacity and actual generation
Interpret the stacked-bar chart of generation mix
Read the carbon intensity line chart
State the caveat that rising renewable share doesn't guarantee falling total emissions

sectionPlan.heading: Where India's electricity came from last year
sectionPlan.job: Directly answer the page question with the 2025 generation mix breakdown.
conceptsToExplain: TWh as a unit of energy
sectionPlan.caveat: The 2025 snapshot doesn't show seasonal variability; hydro generation fluctuates with monsoons.

sectionPlan.heading: How has the generation mix shifted over time?
sectionPlan.job: Show that coal generation has risen to meet soaring demand, while clean generation has also grown steadily.
conceptsToExplain: Why demand growth is the main driver
The composition of clean sources (hydro, nuclear, solar, wind)
sectionPlan.caveat: The chart groups many clean sources; the rise in solar generation is not visible separately here.

sectionPlan.heading: What has India actually built? Installed capacity by source
sectionPlan.job: Reveal the rapid build-out of renewable capacity, especially solar, which hints at future shifts.
lockedNumbersToUse: Fossil fuels electricity installed capacity, latest

Why renewable capacity is growing faster

sectionPlan.heading: What share of electricity actually comes from renewables?

conceptsToExplain: Why hydro historically dominated renewable share

sectionPlan.heading: Is the grid actually getting cleaner per unit?
sectionPlan.job: Explain what carbon intensity means and that it fell from 740 gCO2/kWh in 2000 to 670 in 2025.
conceptsToExplain: Carbon intensity as grams of CO2 per kilowatt-hour
Why intensity can fall even as total emissions rise
sectionPlan.caveat: Lower intensity doesn't mean lower total emissions if demand grows fast.

chartPlan.title: Where India's electricity came from last year
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It directly answers the page question by showing the single-year generation mix.
chartPlan.howToRead: Each bar segment is a source's share of total generation; the whole bar equals 100% of 2025 demand.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse share percentages with absolute TWh; the total bar height is 2,082.82 TWh.

chartPlan.title: Coal, clean power and gas over time
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It reveals the evolution of the fuel mix and the persistent dominance of coal even as clean sources rise.
chartPlan.howToRead: The lines show TWh generated each year; coal is the top line, clean the middle, gas the bottom.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume clean growth has displaced coal; both have grown, driven by demand.

chartPlan.title: What India has built: installed capacity by source
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It shows the faster-moving capacity build-out, which feeds into future generation.
chartPlan.howToRead: Multiple lines show capacity in million kilowatts; solar line rises steeply after 2010.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't equate capacity with generation; solar capacity generates only ~20-25% of its rated value annualized.

chartPlan.title: Renewables' share of generation
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It isolates the headline green yardstick: the percentage of electricity that is renewable.
chartPlan.howToRead: A single line tracking percentage from 1985 to 2025; it ended at 24.1%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This share includes large hydro; exclude large hydro and the share would be lower.

chartPlan.title: The grid is getting cleaner per unit
chartPlan.whyShowThis: It measures progress in lowering emissions per kilowatt-hour, the key climate metric.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line shows grams of CO2 per kWh; it started at 740 in 2000 and ended at 670 in 2025.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't interpret a falling line as a sign that total emissions are falling; they rose 264% in the same period.

visualizationDecisions.reason: A single stacked bar for the latest year is most readable on mobile.
Lines show the trend clearly; using 3 lines avoids clutter on a phone screen.
Multiple lines let readers compare build-outs directly; sparse ticks keep it mobile-friendly.
A single line trend is the simplest way to show a share over time.
A falling line is intuitive for improvement; works well on mobile.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A terawatt-hour is a unit of energy equal to 1 billion kilowatt-hours. It measures the total electricity generated or consumed in a year.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: All generation numbers are in TWh to show the massive scale of India's electricity system.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The maximum power a plant can produce, measured in megawatts or million kilowatts. It's like the horsepower of an engine, not the distance it travels.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The capacity chart shows how fast India is adding renewable hardware, even though generation lags.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The breakdown of total electricity produced by fuel type, coal, gas, hydro, nuclear, solar, wind, etc.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The page question directly asks for this mix, and the stacked bar answers it.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The amount of carbon dioxide emitted per unit of electricity generated, measured in grams of CO2 per kilowatt-hour. Lower is cleaner.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It shows whether the grid is getting cleaner even as total electricity use grows.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The actual output of a plant over a year divided by its theoretical maximum output if it ran 24/7. Solar typically has a low capacity factor because the sun doesn't shine at night.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: It explains why solar capacity doesn't instantly translate into the same share of generation.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number
Round numbers using displayValue when present

lockedNumbers.label: Fossil fuels electricity installed capacity, latest
Fossil fuels electricity installed capacity, earliest

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
